title: SEGMENT 4 — Examples of stuck gas pedals

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Examples of stuck gas pedals
Let's make the gas-pedal idea concrete with four real examples.

Every one of these is a gene you might have heard mentioned in a news article about a cancer drug, and I want you to leave this segment knowing roughly what each one is and what it does when it goes wrong.
RAS.

This is probably the most famous oncogene in all of biology, and it shows up mutated in something like a quarter of all human cancers — including most pancreatic cancers, many colon cancers, and a chunk of lung cancers.

The normal job of RAS is to sit just inside the cell membrane and act as a switch in a signaling chain — when a growth signal arrives from outside, RAS gets flipped to its "on" state and passes the message downstream.
Crucially, normal RAS flips itself off again after a moment, like a doorbell button that springs back out.
Mutated RAS, in cancer, can't flip itself off.
The button stays pressed.
The growth signal is delivered continuously, whether or not anything outside the cell is asking for it.
The pedal is stuck.
RAS was, for decades, considered "undruggable.
" The protein is small, smooth, and offers nothing convenient for a drug molecule to grab onto.
It was a famous failure of the field, year after year.
Only in the last few years have we finally cracked one specific mutated version, called KRAS G12C, with drugs like sotorasib.
That's a genuine triumph and we'll come back to it, but the broader RAS story is still mostly: we know exactly what's wrong, and we still can't fix it well.
MYC.
MYC is a transcription factor — a protein that goes into the nucleus and turns on the expression of many other genes, particularly genes involved in cell growth and division.
Normal MYC turns on briefly when the cell is supposed to grow, then shuts off.

Cancer cells often have MYC turned on permanently, by various tricks — sometimes the gene gets duplicated many times, sometimes it gets shuffled next to a regulatory region that keeps it constantly active.

A famous example is Burkitt lymphoma, where MYC gets translocated — physically moved by a chromosomal rearrangement — to sit next to an immune-cell regulatory element that blares "on" at full volume in B cells.
The result is a B-cell cancer that grows astonishingly fast.
MYC is even harder to drug than RAS — it's a floppy, disordered protein with no good binding pocket — but it's central to so many cancers that the field keeps trying.
EGFR.
The epidermal growth factor receptor.

Picture a protein that sits in the cell membrane with one part sticking outside, waiting for a growth signal, and another part dangling inside the cell, ready to fire off a downstream cascade when the outside part gets pinged.

In certain lung cancers — especially in nonsmokers and especially in patients of East Asian descent — the EGFR gene gets mutated so that the inside part starts firing without needing to be pinged from outside.
It's a doorbell that rings itself.
EGFR is much more druggable than RAS, because the firing mechanism is a well-shaped pocket called a kinase domain, and we're good at making small molecules that fit into kinase pockets.
Drugs like gefitinib, erlotinib, and osimertinib do exactly this — they slot into the kinase pocket and silence the rogue receptor.
BCR-ABL.
This is the one that founded the entire field of targeted cancer therapy, and we'll spend an entire segment on it later, so I'll just sketch it here.

In a leukemia called chronic myeloid leukemia, CML, a piece of chromosome 9 swaps with a piece of chromosome 22, fusing two genes that were never meant to meet — a gene called BCR and a gene called ABL.
The fusion protein, BCR-ABL, is a permanently-on kinase that drives the leukemia.
The fused chromosome is so distinctive under a microscope that pathologists named it the Philadelphia chromosome decades before anyone knew what it did molecularly.
The drug imatinib — brand name Gleevec — is designed to fit into the ABL kinase pocket and shut the fusion protein off.
We'll come back to why this drug changed everything.
Notice the pattern across all four: gas pedals stuck on.
The cell receives, generates, or transmits a continuous growth signal that it shouldn't be getting.
And for each of them, the therapeutic strategy is conceptually the same: find a drug that physically obstructs the stuck pedal.
